text: In the left lower quadrant of the abdomen, the surgeon carefully separates adhesions caused by diverticulitis. The small bowel is detached from the anterior abdominal wall and the inflamed sigmoid colon. Thick fibrous adhesions between the sigmoid colon and the anterior abdominal wall are meticulously separated to prevent fecal contamination. Inflamed diverticula in the sigmoid colon are visible, along with the tinea coli.

The surgeon dissects the right leaf of the mesosigmoid, identifying the sacral promontory. Above this landmark, the mesocolon is divided, revealing the sigmoid branches of the inferior mesenteric artery. Each of the three branches is carefully isolated, clipped, and divided using harmonic shears.

The surgeon continues the dissection downwards towards the rectosigmoid junction, reaching the proposed site of division. The mesosigmoid is dissected posteriorly up to the third piece of the sacrum, with the posterior wall of the rectosigmoid clearly visible. The inferior mesenteric artery is preserved to maintain blood supply to the upper rectum, reducing the risk of anastomotic leak.

The surgeon ensures the caudal end of the dissection includes all diverticula, then turns the peritoneal incision anteriorly and to the left to divide the mesocolon. The muscular fibers of the anterior surface of the rectosigmoid are visible, along with the pulsations of the preserved inferior mesenteric artery. The dissection progresses to the peritoneum on the left lateral pelvic wall.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the rectosigmoid junction and carries the incision upwards along the left paracolic gutter, mobilizing the lower portion of the left colon. The dissection is then directed downwards, where the gonadal vessels are identified. The left ureter is carefully identified and safeguarded, with visible uretic peristalsis.

The surgeon continues the incision on the left side of the rectosigmoid towards the pelvis, joining it with the previous incision from the right side. The posterior surface of the rectosigmoid is now fully mobilized and free from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses an echelon-sixty stapler with a green load to divide the circumferentially dissected rectosigmoid, after removing surrounding fat. The colon is exteriorized through an incision in the left iliac fossa, and the specimen is excised. The anvil of the stapler is placed, a purse string is applied, and the colon is pushed inward.
A 33 French SDH stapler is introduced trans-anally, with the spike advancing through the staple line. The rectal wall is visibly supported as the spike is maneuvered into position.

The anvil of the stapler is locked into place at the sigmoid colon. The ends of the sigmoid and rectum are approximated by closing the stapler, ensuring no fat or intestine is trapped between them. The stapler is activated, completing the anastomosis, and is then gradually removed. The anastomosis is tested by injecting saline from below to check for leaks.
